requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must elect to amortize startup and organizational costs exceeding immediate expense limit over 180 months beginning with month business begins, allowing systematic recovery of startup investments
criteria: Startup costs incurred before business begins active operations
Qualified startup costs: investigation expenses, creation costs, organizational expenses for entity formation
Can immediately expense up to $5,000 startup costs; $5,000 organizational costs (separate limits)
Dollar-for-dollar reduction in $5,000 limit when total startup costs exceed $50,000
Costs exceeding immediate expense limit amortized over 180 months (15 years) starting when business begins
Must affirmatively elect amortization on timely filed return (including extensions) for first year business active
Examples: market surveys, consultant fees, legal/accounting for setup, temporary director fees, training costs
Election applies separately to startup costs and organizational costs

startup_cost_rules.immediate_expense: Up to $5,000 startup costs + $5,000 organizational costs deductible immediately
startup_cost_rules.phase_out: Dollar-for-dollar reduction when total costs exceed $50,000; eliminated at $55,000+
startup_cost_rules.amortization_period: 180 months (15 years) for costs exceeding immediate expense limit
startup_cost_rules.qualified_costs: Investigation, creation, organizational expenses; not acquisition or long-term asset costs
startup_cost_rules.election_required: Must elect on timely filed return or automatic election applies via Reg §1.195-1(b)
startup_cost_rules.separate_tracking: Startup costs and organizational costs tracked and elected separately

restrictions: Costs incurred before business begins; cannot amortize costs after business starts operating
Failed to elect on timely return: costs not deductible until business sold or abandoned
Research and experimentation costs subject to separate rules under IRC §174 (must capitalize and amortize over 5 years)
Acquisition costs for specific business or assets not qualified startup costs
Interest, taxes, and costs that would be deductible anyway not eligible for startup election
Partnership organizational costs include formation, legal fees, accounting fees; not syndication costs
If business never begins, startup costs may be deductible as loss if qualified investigation expenses
State tax treatment varies; some states don't conform to federal immediate expense or amortization rules
